title: The Petrodollar is Dying. Nobody is Announcing it.
author: Anhad Singh
excerpt: A deep dive into the 1974 US-Saudi handshake, the weaponization of the dollar, and the shift toward a multipolar energy economy.
content:
In 1974, the United States and Saudi Arabia made a deal that changed the world. America would provide military protection and weapons to the Kingdom. In return, Saudi Arabia would price its oil exclusively in US dollars and invest its extra money in American Treasury bonds. This arrangement was not a treaty and did not go through Congress. It was a handshake between two governments that understood their exchange clearly. Other OPEC nations followed suit. Just like that, the dollar became the standard in global energy.

To understand why this matters, you need to see what it created. Every country needed oil. Therefore, every country needed dollars. This meant every country had to hold dollars in reserve. As a result, America could print money, run deficits, and fund wars in a way no other country could because the demand for its currency was guaranteed. This demand was driven not just by the strength of its economy but also by the price of crude oil.

But before Saudi Arabia, there was Iran.

In the decades before 1979, Iran under the Shah was one of America’s key allies in the Middle East. American and British oil companies had deep ties to Iran's oil infrastructure going back to the early 20th century. The 1953 coup, led by the CIA and British intelligence, restored the Shah after Prime Minister Mosaddegh tried to nationalise Iranian oil. America was not protecting democracy; it was securing access to Iranian oil. The Shah maintained oil production while Washington supported his regime. It was a smooth arrangement until it wasn’t.

The 1979 revolution changed everything. Ayatollah Khomeini’s government expelled the Americans, took hostages, and made confrontation with Washington central to its ideology. America responded with sanctions that have just been lifted by Trump as a cry for help. Over the past four decades, Iran has been mostly cut off from the dollar system, which is like being cut off from the global economy. In this isolation, Iran did something most sanctioned countries do not do. It prepared.

This is where the Strait of Hormuz comes in.

Twenty percent of the world’s oil passes through this waterway, which is only 33 Km wide at its narrowest point. Iran sits on one side. The entire global energy supply chain runs through it. For years, this was a theoretical issue. Last year, it became real. Iranian proxies and direct attacks brought the world closer to a closure of Hormuz than it had been in decades. Shipping costs soared. Insurance premiums for vessels in the Gulf became nearly unbearable. The world got a glimpse of what Iran had been building quietly for twenty years: coordinated pressure, asymmetric capability, and a willingness to endure hardship for leverage.

Now Khamenei is dead. The next leader is not likely to seek diplomacy. Iran has seen America impose sanctions, assassinate its generals, sabotage its nuclear facilities, and support all its regional foes. The new leadership doesn’t need to create anger. It has two decades worth already built up.

Trump’s return to maximum pressure has not stabilised the situation. He made it worse. Threatening to bomb Iran’s oil infrastructure may sound like a deterrent, but Iran’s response to an existential threat has never been retreat. Instead, it is likely to escalate through every proxy and chokepoint it has developed over twenty years. Professor Jiang Xueqin and others modeling this situation agree. A ground invasion or direct attack on Iran won’t end the conflict, it will spark a regional unraveling that the fragile global economy, weakened by AI speculation, rising debt, and post-pandemic issues, is not ready to handle.

Meanwhile, Saudi Arabia quietly allowed its petrodollar agreement with America to expire last year. There was no announcement. The deal simply lapsed. Riyadh is now selling oil to China in yuan. It is balancing its relationships, keeping one foot in Washington and one in Beijing. It has seen Iran suffer from reliance on one side and intends to avoid that mistake.

Gold purchases by central banks reached record levels in 2022 and 2023. China, Russia, India, and several emerging economies are accumulating gold with unusual urgency. The reason is straightforward. When America froze Russia’s dollar reserves after Ukraine, every government that was not a close American ally got the same message. Dollar reserves are not neutral; they are a weapon. Gold is not.

The petrodollar is not collapsing tomorrow. The dollar still holds dominance. However, the foundations that guaranteed that dominance, including the Saudi deal, Iranian compliance, and the steady American naval presence in the Gulf, are slowly breaking apart. The system is not ending with a declaration. It is ending as most things do: slowly, then all at once.

title: Weaponising Energy: How the Strait of Hormuz Became the Epicentre for Economic Warfare
author: Anhad Singh
excerpt: An exploration of the Strait of Hormuz as a strategic 'kill button' for global energy and the shift toward a multipolar world order.
content:
"Whoever controls the flow of energy controls the future of the nations".

The Strait of Hormuz has become a household name by this point of time in the world. But why is it so important? Why does Iran control it? What is its relation to global markets, world economies and energy resources? Well the simple answer in layman terms is that it's a global chokepoint that Iran has taken control over, whereas after doing deeper research I have found that it is not just a chokepoint, its the kill button, and Iran has strategically got its index on it.

Allow me to share with you some background information. One-Fifth of the world's oil passes through the Strait of Hormuz. Along with that China signed a 25 year agreement in 2021 that China will invest $400 billion in Iran’s economy in exchange for a steady and heavily discounted supply of oil from Iran. Under this agreement China gets a minimum guaranteed discount of 12% on oil prices, plus an additional 6-8% discount for risk.

THE PROVOCATION THAT CHANGED EVERYTHING

Now, this didn't happen in a vacuum. Trump's administration, along with Netanyahu and Israel, had been escalating tensions with Iran for years. When Trump withdrew from the Iran nuclear deal in 2018, he imposed what they called "maximum pressure" sanctions on Iran. This wasn't just diplomacy it was economic warfare. Israel, backed by Trump, launched strikes and conducted covert operations against Iranian nuclear facilities. These weren't subtle moves. They were deliberate provocations designed to destabilize Iran.

But here's where it gets interesting. Instead of backing down, Iran made a calculated strategic move. They realized that the Western world was trying to suffocate their economy through sanctions. So what did they do? They turned to China. They deepened their alliance and locked in that 25 year deal. It wasn't just about survival it was about flipping the script. Iran understood that if they couldn't compete in the Western-dominated global economy, they would become indispensable to the rising power of the East. China needed oil. Iran had oil. And most importantly, Iran controlled the Strait of Hormuz.

This was Iran's answer to Trump and Netanyahu's aggression. This was Iran saying: "You want to play economic warfare? Fine. But remember, 20% of the world's oil comes through my waters."

WHO ACTUALLY OWNS THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ?

Here's where international law gets interesting, and frankly, where most people get it wrong. Iran doesn't actually own the Strait of Hormuz outright. According to international maritime law, specifically the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), every country has the right to claim territorial waters extending 12 nautical miles (roughly 22 kilometers) from its coastline.

The Strait of Hormuz is about 33 nautical miles wide at its narrowest point. This means that both Iran and Oman have territorial waters within the strait. Iran controls the southern half, while Oman controls the northern half. Technically, large portions of the strait are international waters, and ships have the "right of innocent passage".

But here's the catch: the sea on the side of Oman is unstable, it has terrible turbulence which do not allow ships to pass through it. Whereas Iran sits on the southern shore of the strait, its waters are calm and allow ships to pass through, it also controls the islands within it, and has military capabilities that can reach into those international waters. It's not ownership in the legal sense, but it's control in the practical sense.

THE PETRODOLLAR AND ITS DECLINING POWER

For decades, the United States maintained its global dominance through a simple but brilliant mechanism: oil is traded in US dollars. This meant that every country needed dollars to buy oil. This gave America enormous power. It allowed them to print money, to run massive deficits, and to sanction countries at will. It was the backbone of American economic dominance.

But this system is crumbling. When China and Iran trade oil now, they're increasingly doing it in Chinese yuan instead of US dollars. This is revolutionary. It means China doesn't need dollars to buy Iranian oil. It means Iran can access revenue without going through the dollar system. It's a direct challenge to American economic dominance.

THE TWO MILLION DOLLAR TOLL AND TRUMP'S BLOCKADE

Iran proposed a toll system: a fee that every ship passing through the Strait of Hormuz would have to pay. We're talking about 2 million dollars per vessel. Trump blocked it because if Iran successfully implemented a toll system, it would be a public admission that Iran controls the strait. It would shatter the myth of American power.

This is economic warfare in its purest form. Trump and the West were trying to suffocate Iran's economy through sanctions. Iran was trying to leverage its geographic position to extract wealth from global commerce. As a result trump stated that he would essentially block the blockade done by Iran, which was later proved substantial when the US navy allowed Chinese ships to pass through.

THE BIGGER PICTURE

What we're witnessing with the Strait of Hormuz isn't just a regional conflict. It's a larger shift happening in global geopolitics. The unipolar American world order is declining. The petrodollar is weakening. China is rising. And Iran, despite being sanctioned and isolated, has managed to position itself as a critical player in this new world order.

Iran didn't start this conflict. Trump and Netanyahu did, with their aggressive policies and military provocations. But Iran responded intelligently. They didn't try to compete with America on America's terms. Instead, they moved to a different playing field entirely.

The Strait of Hormuz has become the epicentre of economic warfare because energy is power, and whoever controls energy controls the future of nations.

title: The New Oil Is Not Data. It's Electricity.
author: Anhad Singh
excerpt: AI may define this decade, but the bottleneck is not software — it is power. Why utilities, grids, and energy infrastructure may be the asymmetric trade.
content:
Every cycle has a phrase that becomes so overused it loses all meaning.

In the 2000s it was globalization.

In the 2010s it was data.

Today it is AI.

The problem with investment manias is not that they are wrong. The problem is that they are often directionally right and financially disastrous at the same time.

The internet changed the world. Most internet stocks still got obliterated.

Railroads transformed America. Most railroad investors went bankrupt.

AI will likely be the defining technology of this decade. That does not automatically mean AI companies will be the defining investments.

Investors are asking the wrong question.

The question is not who builds the best model.

The question is what becomes scarce if AI succeeds.

History offers a useful framework.

When automobiles emerged, the winning trade was not necessarily car manufacturers. Hundreds of them disappeared. The more durable winners were the businesses that enabled an automotive society. Oil companies, tire manufacturers, road construction firms, insurance providers, and suburban real estate all became beneficiaries of a structural shift.

Technological revolutions rarely reward the obvious participants. They reward the bottlenecks.

Today, the bottleneck is electricity.

Every major AI model ultimately reduces to one thing: massive computational power. Computational power requires data centers. Data centers require energy. Lots of it.

For the better part of two decades, developed economies operated under the assumption that electricity demand would remain relatively flat. Efficiency improvements offset incremental consumption. Energy became a utility rather than a growth industry.

That assumption is now breaking.

The average large AI training run consumes exponentially more power than traditional software workloads. Hyperscalers are investing hundreds of billions of dollars into infrastructure that requires a physical backbone. Unlike software, electrons cannot be scaled through clever code.

They must be generated.

This creates a fascinating investment setup.

Markets have spent the last three years obsessing over AI applications while largely ignoring the industries required to sustain them.

Utilities, power transmission networks, grid modernization companies, natural gas infrastructure, nuclear developers, and industrial equipment manufacturers suddenly find themselves at the center of the most important technological trend of our generation.

This is not a temporary phenomenon.

Civilizations are ultimately constrained by energy.

Economic growth is simply humanity's ability to consume more energy and direct it toward increasingly productive outcomes.

The Industrial Revolution was an energy story.

The rise of America was an energy story.

China's manufacturing dominance was an energy story.

AI is becoming an energy story.

The market's fixation on software obscures a deeper reality. Every technological leap eventually collides with physical limits.

Cloud computing required data centers.

E-commerce required logistics networks.

Streaming required broadband infrastructure.

AI requires power.

The most interesting opportunities often emerge where narratives and fundamentals diverge.

Narratives currently favor software.

Fundamentals increasingly favor infrastructure.

This does not mean AI companies are poor investments. Some will become enormously valuable. But markets have a tendency to crowd into obvious stories while neglecting the second-order consequences.

Second-order effects are where asymmetric returns usually live.

Investors should ask themselves a simple question.

If AI adoption doubles over the next five years, who benefits regardless of which model wins?

The answer is unlikely to be found in another chatbot.

It might be found in the companies ensuring the lights stay on.

For decades, energy was viewed as a legacy industry.

The next decade may prove it was merely sleeping.

And if history is any guide, the biggest fortunes are often made by identifying the bottleneck before everyone else realizes it exists.

title: The Middle East Ceasefire Changes Less Than People Think
author: Anhad Singh
excerpt: Markets love events. History loves structures. Why the Iran–Israel ceasefire is a pause — not a resolution — in the contest for regional order.
content:
Markets love events.

History loves structures.

The difference between the two explains why investors and policymakers consistently misunderstand geopolitics.

The recent ceasefire between Iran and Israel has been interpreted as a turning point. Commentators are already debating winners and losers. Some see de-escalation. Others see deterrence restored. A few believe the region has stepped back from the brink.

They may all be missing the larger picture.

The ceasefire is important.

The forces that produced the conflict are far more important.

To understand what happens next, it is necessary to understand what actually changed and what remained exactly the same.

For decades, the Middle East operated under a relatively stable strategic framework. America acted as the primary security guarantor. Regional powers competed, but largely within boundaries enforced by overwhelming US military dominance.

That order has been gradually eroding.

The wars in Iraq and Afghanistan weakened American appetite for large-scale intervention. China's economic rise shifted Washington's strategic focus toward Asia. Regional actors increasingly recognized that they would need to navigate a world with less direct American involvement.

This created a vacuum.

Vacuums rarely stay empty.

Iran expanded its network of proxies across the region. Saudi Arabia pursued a more independent foreign policy. Turkey became increasingly assertive. Israel faced a growing number of security challenges that no longer fit traditional military frameworks.

The recent conflict emerged from this changing landscape.

Many observers frame the confrontation as a military dispute.

It is better understood as a contest over regional order.

The central question is not who won a particular exchange.

The central question is who gets to shape the Middle East after the American unipolar moment.

That question remains unanswered.

The ceasefire therefore represents a pause rather than a resolution.

History offers numerous examples.

The Korean War ended with an armistice rather than a peace treaty.

The Cold War experienced repeated periods of de-escalation without resolving its underlying rivalry.

India and Pakistan have repeatedly stepped back from escalation while preserving the structural conditions that make future crises possible.

Ceasefires stop violence.

They rarely eliminate incentives.

Those incentives remain firmly in place today.

Iran still seeks strategic depth.

Israel still seeks security guarantees.

Arab states still seek stability without becoming dependent on any single external power.

The United States still wants regional influence while reducing regional commitments.

China still wants economic access without security responsibilities.

None of these objectives disappeared because of a ceasefire.

The economic implications are equally significant.

The global economy has become remarkably dependent on a small number of geopolitical chokepoints.

The Strait of Hormuz remains one of the most strategically important waterways in the world. A substantial portion of global energy flows through a region where miscalculation can occur within hours.

Markets periodically forget this reality.

Then a crisis emerges and everyone remembers.

The broader lesson is that globalization is evolving rather than disappearing.

The era of frictionless globalization was built upon assumptions of stability.

Those assumptions are increasingly being challenged.

Supply chains are becoming strategic assets.

Energy security is becoming national security.

Industrial policy is returning after decades of neglect.

Governments are prioritizing resilience alongside efficiency.

The Middle East sits at the center of these shifts.

What happens there no longer stays there.

A missile strike can affect energy prices in Europe.

A shipping disruption can impact manufacturing in Asia.

A regional escalation can influence inflation expectations in the United States.

Geopolitics has become a macroeconomic variable again.

For much of the post-Cold War era, investors could largely ignore it.

That luxury is disappearing.

The most likely outcome is neither permanent peace nor immediate war.

It is prolonged competition under a fragile equilibrium.

Periods of calm will alternate with periods of confrontation.

Markets will repeatedly mistake temporary stabilization for permanent resolution.

They always do.

The deeper story is not the ceasefire itself.

The deeper story is the transition from one global order to another.

The post-1991 world was defined by overwhelming American dominance.

The next world will likely be defined by multiple centers of power, overlapping spheres of influence, and constant negotiation between competing interests.

The Middle East is not separate from that transition.

It is one of the places where that transition is being tested in real time.

That is why the ceasefire matters.

Not because it ended a conflict.

But because it revealed the shape of the conflicts still to come.
